sections.heading: What are Android Permissions?
sections.content: Android permissions are a security mechanism that protects sensitive user data and device features from unauthorized access. Apps must declare which permissions they need in the AndroidManifest.xml, and for dangerous permissions, must also request them at runtime from the user. Permissions are grouped into three categories: normal permissions (granted automatically at install), dangerous permissions (require explicit user approval at runtime), and special permissions (require the user to navigate to system settings to grant). Since Android 6.0 (API 23), dangerous permissions must be requested at runtime — users can grant or deny them individually and revoke them at any time from device settings.

rows.revokable: Yes — from app settings at any time

rows.user_prompt: No standard dialog — app redirects user to Settings

rows.granted_at: Automatically if apps share the same signing certificate
rows.examples: Custom permissions defined between apps from the same developer

sections.heading: Declaring Permissions in the Manifest
items: All permissions must be declared in AndroidManifest.xml using <uses-permission>
Example: <uses-permission android:name="android.permission.CAMERA" />
Normal permissions are automatically granted — no runtime request needed
Dangerous permissions declared in manifest still require runtime request on API 23+
Use android:maxSdkVersion to restrict permission declaration to specific API levels
Example: <uses-permission android:name="android.permission.READ_EXTERNAL_STORAGE" android:maxSdkVersion="32" />
For Android 13+, use granular media permissions: READ_MEDIA_IMAGES, READ_MEDIA_VIDEO, READ_MEDIA_AUDIO instead of READ_EXTERNAL_STORAGE

sections.heading: Requesting Runtime Permissions
sections.content: Runtime permissions must be requested using the Activity Result API introduced in AndroidX. The recommended approach is to use ActivityResultContracts.RequestPermission() for a single permission or ActivityResultContracts.RequestMultiplePermissions() for multiple permissions at once. Always check if the permission is already granted before requesting it using ContextCompat.checkSelfPermission(). If the user has previously denied the permission, use shouldShowRequestPermissionRationale() to determine if you should show an explanation before requesting again.

sections.heading: Runtime Permission Request Flow
items: Step 1: Check if permission is already granted: ContextCompat.checkSelfPermission(context, Manifest.permission.CAMERA) == PackageManager.PERMISSION_GRANTED
Step 2: If granted, proceed with the feature directly
Step 3: If not granted, check if rationale should be shown: ActivityCompat.shouldShowRequestPermissionRationale(activity, Manifest.permission.CAMERA)
Step 4: If rationale needed, show a dialog explaining why the permission is needed before requesting
Step 5: Register launcher: val cameraPermissionLauncher = registerForActivityResult(ActivityResultContracts.RequestPermission()) { isGranted -> if (isGranted) openCamera() else handleDenied() }
Step 6: Launch the request: cameraPermissionLauncher.launch(Manifest.permission.CAMERA)
Step 7: Handle the result in the callback — isGranted: Boolean
Step 8: If permanently denied (shouldShowRequestPermissionRationale returns false after denial), guide user to app settings: Intent(Settings.ACTION_APPLICATION_DETAILS_SETTINGS)

rows.use_case: Reading or modifying the user's contact list
Accessing images from the media store
Accessing video files from the media store
Accessing audio files from the media store
Scanning for and connecting to Bluetooth devices
Displaying push notifications to the user

sections.heading: Requesting Multiple Permissions
items: Use ActivityResultContracts.RequestMultiplePermissions() to request several permissions at once

val multiplePermissionsLauncher = registerForActivityResult(ActivityResultContracts.RequestMultiplePermissions()) { permissions -> val cameraGranted = permissions[Manifest.permission.CAMERA] == true; val audioGranted = permissions[Manifest.permission.RECORD_AUDIO] == true }
Launch with an array: multiplePermissionsLauncher.launch(arrayOf(Manifest.permission.CAMERA, Manifest.permission.RECORD_AUDIO))
The result is a Map<String, Boolean> — each permission mapped to its grant status
Check each permission individually in the result map
Group related permissions logically — only request permissions needed for the current user action
Avoid requesting all permissions at app launch — this is a common UX anti-pattern

sections.heading: Permission Best Practices
items: Request permissions in context — ask when the user triggers the feature that needs it, not at app launch
Explain why — use a rationale dialog before requesting if shouldShowRequestPermissionRationale() returns true
Fail gracefully — if permission is denied, degrade functionality rather than crashing or blocking the user
Never block the UI indefinitely waiting for permission — provide a fallback path
Handle permanent denial — if the user selects 'Don't ask again', guide them to Settings to manually grant
Re-check permissions in onResume() — users can revoke permissions while the app is backgrounded
Use the principle of least privilege — only request permissions that are strictly necessary
Prefer scoped storage APIs and media picker over broad storage permissions
Use the Photo Picker (ActivityResultContracts.PickVisualMedia) — grants access to selected media without needing READ_MEDIA_IMAGES

rows.action: Show rationale explaining the benefit of the permission, then re-request
Show a dialog explaining the situation and provide a button to open app Settings

rows.scenario: Permission revoked while app is running
rows.action: Re-check in onResume() — SecurityException will be thrown if you use a revoked permission
Proceed with the feature — no action needed

sections.heading: Location Permission — Special Considerations
items: ACCESS_FINE_LOCATION (GPS) and ACCESS_COARSE_LOCATION (network) are in the same permission group
Request both together to let the user choose the precision level on Android 12+
Background location (ACCESS_BACKGROUND_LOCATION) must be requested separately — after foreground location is granted
Background location requires a strong justification for Play Store approval
On Android 10+ (API 29+), always request foreground location first, then background separately
Android 12 (API 31+) introduced approximate location — users can grant approximate even if you request precise
Always handle the case where the user grants only approximate location instead of precise
Use FusedLocationProviderClient from Google Play Services for efficient, battery-friendly location access

sections.heading: Summary
sections.content: Android's permission system protects sensitive user data and device features by requiring explicit user consent for dangerous permissions at runtime. Always declare permissions in the manifest, check before requesting using ContextCompat.checkSelfPermission(), use the Activity Result API for requesting, and handle all outcomes — granted, denied, and permanently denied. Request permissions contextually when the user triggers the relevant feature, provide clear rationale when needed, and gracefully degrade functionality when permissions are denied. For special permissions like overlay or full storage access, redirect users to the relevant system settings screen. Follow the principle of least privilege and prefer privacy-preserving alternatives like the Photo Picker wherever possible.
